TRISH CHAP BOOKS, SONG BOOKS AND BALLADS.

A very interesting article on ‘¢ Irish Chap Books,”’ by . Mr. Marshall,
.u[ Bolfast, appeared in No. 12, in which he also referred to Song Books
l.:j " .nd Ballads. These publications, as indicated in the title of this con-
tribution and dealt with in BMr. Marshall’s article, form quite separate
and distinct classes, varying thoroughly from one another, particularly
! the first from the other two. The w riter of the article in question has
Kindly explained to me that he only wrote the article as an introduction
L-"‘? to an interesting subject, and with his permission I wish to refer a

.;__hntle further to it. What he has written is of distinct value, but 1

l_!thmk the subject is one big enough and 111terestmg enough to be dealt

_f~‘~ with more fully, and I would suggest to whoever may undertake it,
to keop the subject of Chap Books quite distinct from the others.

" Cuir Booxs.—When Chap Books were first printed in Ireland I do

: uut knuw for certain, but I have seen what might B,e classed as such at

i the very end of the 17th or begnmmg of the 1Sth century, during which

i lﬂtter period they became very common.b They were pubhshed not

*““‘11}' 111 Dublin, but in various other tow ns as Mr. Marshall states, and. |

uf
course, varied much in size and subject. Ge‘nerally they were 12mos.

i ‘1088 but somotimes Svos.  Some were popular narratwes, some
4. olal t{ﬂBS, some religious ﬁnd hlstorlcal others facetmus, a. few;
q«eﬁaﬁlcﬂl They were very badly printed as a rule, and 111ust1'a.ted often

‘&F. !r.‘:}f a8 forming such a striking contrast to the splendld 111ustra.—-

the different character of those printed in various towns. No doyy
the travelling hawkers who brought these books to the various fairg
and markets for sale would only bring such as were suited to the diffg,
ent tastes of the various places they went to. But the subject is gz,

gether too big to be dealt with within the limits of a short article Jj,
this

Soxe Books.—I think Mr. Marshall did not sufficiently distinguig,

these from the Street Ballads or Ballad Sheets. The Song Books cop.
sisted of a sheet folded so as to form what may be called a ‘‘ duodecimg”
of 8 pp. Generally there was a rude woodcut on the front page, some
times there was nothing at all like a title page, but sometimes there wa
a regular title page with an imprint giving the name of the town and th

printer’s name, and even the year. These Song Books I presume mus |

have been sold for a penny or twopence and probably reproduced fron |
time to time according as some event dealt with in them again occasmned
a2 demand for them in any particular place. Here, too, the differert
tastes of the different localities is indicated. There is a very fine wl
lection of these Song Books in the British Museum, as Mr. Marshil

points out. I have a good many too, mostly bound in volumes, o }
put in cases according to their locality, and it 1is stf-iking to obserr
that some towns seem never to have indulged in these Song Books a |
all, whilst others did so frequently. Nor were these Song Books cot ;
fined to Dublin or Belfast, Limerick or such large towns, for we find |
them published even in small towns. Indeed to deal with this subjest |

fully one would require a volume, which should contain facsimiles t |

THE HARVARD CHAP BOOKS.

We nre indebted to the Librarian of Harvard University for a copy
of the fine ‘¢ Catalogue of the Chap Books and Broadsides ” in that
famous library, referred to at p. 12, vol. II.; and present our readers
with a synopsis of its contents, as far as they relate to the productions
of Trish presses. The only towns represented are Dublin and Belfast,
Af which the former has three titles to its credit, and the latter no less
than forty-one, or to be more correct, forty, for one of the items men-
tioned, McSkimin’s Islandmagee Witches, 1822, is nov really a chap
beok. Of the three bearing the Dublin imprint, two are copies of the
same work—¢‘ The Seven Wise Masters,”’ one printed by Fox in 1814,
the other by Warren, so of course, more modern, whilst the third is a
song book containing ‘‘ The Ceolun ’’ and ‘‘ Shawn a Glanna,’”’ printed
hy Corcoran, ~.n. Of those pfinted at Belfast, the first twelve range in
date from 1761 until 1769, and all but one are 16mo., pp. 8, thus coming
under Mr. Dix’s category of song books, as distinct from Chap Books.
They bear no printer’s name, have, as usual, a rough wood-cut on title-
page, and not one of them is included in Anderson’s Catalogue of
Harly Belfast Printed Books.” The first printer’s name to appear 18

- that of James Magee, and the dates of his publications—twenty-seven in

In number—range hetween 1759 and 1769. With two exceptions, they
are 8 page 12 and 16mo. Of all these, only two possess any local asso-

Clations, one containing the songs of ‘‘ The Belfast Damsel ”’ and ** Kll-
levy,” the other ** William Montgomery’s last farewell, who was execu-
ted at Belfast on the 15th of April, 1788, which was a dying speech
and assertion of innocence, in verse. These titles are also absent from o
Antleranp. Under the year 1768 we discover the name of ‘a printer, ;
K Me.Connel , of whom no mention has ever been made before.

JOSEPH D’ARCY SIRR, D.D.

In response to the invitation to supply a memoir of my uncle, D,
D’Arcy Sirr, I send some particulars which I think may be of interest,
He was born in Dublin in 1794, entered Trinity College, 1808 ; becama
B.A. 1812; M.A. 1823; and B.D. and D.D. 1843. He was ordained Dea.
con in 1818 and priest in 1819. In 1820 he was located at Sutton, Win.
chester, probably holding a curacy there. He became successively
Rector of Kilcoleman, Co. Galway, in 1823 ; of Claremorris, Co. Mayo,
in 1841 ; Vicar of Yoxford, in Suffolk, in 1844 ; and Rector of St. Mary,
Spitalfields in 1846. Then, in 1849, he became Rector of Ringwood,
Hants, where he remained until 1859 when he was appointed to the living
of Morstead, Hants. He was Rector here at the time of his death in
1868, and was buried in Morstead Churchyard He was first Hon
Secretary of the Irish Society for Promoting Scriptural Education in the
Irish language, and was intimate with Archbishop Poer le Poer Trench
who was 'closeljr connected wit&h the Society, and a friend of Major Sirr,
his father.

In 1829 Sirr was elected M.R.I.A., and while in the Winchester
Diocese he was appointed Chaplain of the Forces. He had a large circle
of relatives and friends in Ireland with whom he kept in touch after

‘leavmg for Fngland in 1844. Amongst the additional manuscripts 1
the British Museum there is an autograph letter written by him t0
_:SIT' Wllham Betham, Ulster King at Arms, from Dublin Castle in 1834

concermng the advowson of Killuecan in RE the case of Ma‘rqms Clanrk-

carde and the Bishop of Meath. His book-plate is included in the

i Franks Ca]lectlon, Bntlsh Museum. The list of books he pubhshed i8

appended here‘bo As to the life of Dr. James Ussher, Halkett and Laing

‘ Dr. Sirr had a great accumulation of matter, and quite sufficient

for the purpose, if he had gone on with the life of his father of which he
‘ntended the title to have been ¢ Memoir of the Life and Times of

Major Sirr.”’ He was strongly urged to complete it and to correct much

. that was published by those who were misled in what they were told. He R

{ knew, for instancé, that at the time of Hevey’s death whicn occurred in } i

;‘i 1823, he was a pensioner on Major Sirr’s bounty, and that some said 1

;:,: Hevey died at one date and some at another (Davis said ‘‘ he died a

-

pauper lunatic shortly after the trial” in 1802; Madden, in 1853, said
“ he died a few years ago.”’) Dr. Sirr also knew that the Major could
not be technically defended rE Hevey without revealing the names of

| tentious material which it is a pity was subsequently burned with his
manuscripts, etc., at Whiteley’s, where they were then stored. One of

I ought to have written in the flyleaf of the vols. you have that g
were all loose papers found by me amongst rubbish, my father ha"lllgi‘i
burnt as he thought all his more important documents.”’ There i Noth, |

ing to indicate what document is referred to. The ‘‘ Sham Squu-au
gives an account (pp. 273-6) of Sir John Gray’s visit to the Rectory th :
Kilcoleman in 1842, where he spent the day and found Dr. Sirr sorhug

the papers. Gray was horrified to find that one of the loudest dey,. | $
gogues of the day had been a paid informer. In conjunction with th
late Mr. George Norman D’Arcy, Dr. Sirr prepared the catalogue n[
Major Sirr’s pictures. One of the two livings he successively held i |

T T
-, -

Ireland he was appointed to by the Duke of Wellington, or through,j
his offices. Wellington (as Sir Arthur Wellesley,) was acquainted mth}:
Dr. Sirr’s father who helped him to organise the Dublin Police. I 5
Sirr was of a kind and benevolent disposition, and fond of his orch'ud!i
and garden. There must be many references to his name in the lter i
ature of the Irish Society. :

')“” HARRY SIRR, F.R.LB.A.
Tae BArp oF THE LEE.—By the death of Mr. John Fitzgerald, whose
poetical gifts justly won him the title of ‘‘ Bard of the Lee,”” which
took place in his native city, Cork, on the 1dth of May last, Munster’s .
capital lost a most interesting and unique personality, the last of the
galaxy of wits, poets and writers for which Cork was distinguished in the
past century. His great age and being unblest with much of this 1
world’s goods, tended to make him unknown to the present generation

kel

of Cork men, who in any case are devoid ‘of appreciation of hterary

but be also possessed considerable

Fitzgerald was a wood carver by trade,
artistic talent and paintings by him are still to be seen in Cork. A collec-

g, -

| tion of his ‘* Legends, Ballads, and Songs of the Lee,”” was published at i q
» Cork in 1862, many of which had appeared in the local press under the .- g
pseudonym of the ‘‘Cock of St. Barry.”’ They were subsequently reprinted _y --
, . in “ Gems of the Cork Poets,” a Cork printed volume long since oub of i
% | print. He was also a dovoted antiquary and contributed several valu- 3
able papers, topographical and historical to the J ournal of the Oo'rk -1
| Archaeological Society, of which he ‘was o council member. Mr. Fitz- _
gorald’s latest published work was ‘ Echoes of ’98,” Cork, Guy and *‘
Co., 1898, a little volume containing many interesting -r.a-miﬁiﬁﬁenéﬁi;" e s j i

several historical MSS. of great local value. It is gratifying to fing thy ?

an effort is now being made in Cork to perpetuate his memory in
that he himself would have preferred, viz., the publication of a Complete
collection of his poems and prose writings. J. Cy

RoBeErT ELLiort, who died recently at the age of 47, after a lif ol

a fﬂrm ‘

i

varied experiences, will be mourned by many who only knew the may |

by his writings which were as varied as his career. Going to seca g,
cabin boy at 14 years old, he attained his master’s certificate before
retired at 25. Whilst working before the mast in strange seas, he cop.
posed many of the poems which afterwards appeared in ‘¢ Sein Fein,”
and were published last year in a volume entitled, ‘* Matthew J ohnson,
Poet’’ (8vo., pp iv. + 116, Maunsel), two of his baptismal names. H
has left on record many of his maritime adventures in his book with the
attractive title, ‘“ Hi! You!” and another, ‘‘ Act of God.”” Quitting the
sea where he had devoted himself to study with an enthusiasm
rare in our day—only allowing himself four hours sleep out of the twenty-

four—thus becoming master of many tongues, and deeply read in al

art literature, he turned his talents to etching and painting, exhibiting |

at the Royal Academy. Tiring of London he went to Ireland and there
settled down to a patient cultivation of Art and Letters—spreading the
light. Perhaps his best known work, and one of his finest is ‘‘ Art and
Ireland ”’ (8vo., xx. + 314, Sealy Bryers), mainly composed of his con-
tributions to ‘‘ The Leader ’’ and the ‘‘ Irish Rosary.”” In these he held
“aloft the true ideals of the arts and crafts, a guide for all future wor
kers. *‘‘Sein Fein ”’ says he has left behind him a mass of materid
whlch will enhance his already high reputation. el

Hreinrion Zimver.—The death of this dmtmgumhed Celtic scholar
~on 29th July is a severe loss to literature. As a young man he spebt
‘gome time on the Aran Islands and the western sea-board, perfecting
himself in Gaelic. As a result, his publication of the first complete tox
;of the Warzburg Glosses appeared in 1881. This Wa.a followed in 18%
of the laws of the Irish accentt"‘ and moroe recent!

hm views in his ‘¢ Pﬂagms in Irland,’”’ 1901.

‘ATEBBINE DREW --The death of MlﬂB Ca.therme Drew on 26th A“'.

Elté 1st, occurred 1n London. The credit of being the pioneer woman
i qalist has been given to many ladies, but it probably helonged of

of

e did an amount of miscellaneous writing that must have been enor-

i s il
. . mous in 1t8 bulk. She was the daughter of the Rev. Thomas Drew,
h D.D., a noted orator in his day, and sister of the late Sir Thomas Drew,
I R.H.A., of Dublin.

e The Society mnow mnumbers 517 effectual members, representing the
e | flower of Gaelic scholarship thr oughout the world. Its losses by death
n throughout the year have been many, including its esteemed publisher,
¥ Mr. David Nutt, James Trost, the historian of Clare, and Professor
L Zimmern. Its forthcoming volume promises to be of great interest. It

REVIEWS.

T T T e T

We cannot conceive a more appropriate present for a boy, or i
deed for anyone anxious to become acquainted with the literary wealth
that lies hidden in the Celtic lore and legends, than THE Hig
DEeeDps oF Fixx, by T. W. Rolleston (Harrap and Co.). The author, s |
is well known, possesses a fine literary style, and he skilfully presers |
the spirit and atmosphere of the originals, in the tales he has hemai
- brought together from many sources. Mr. Stopford Brooke contributes%

a learned introduction, thus renewing a co-partnership that has pr-|
duced excellent results in the past, and we hope shall again. The s |
teen illustration in colour by Stephen Reid; are real works of ar!
beautifully reproduced. A word of praise is due to the spirited enter
prise of the publisher in issuing such works as this, and its companio
volume, ‘“ The Hound of Ulster,”” by Miss Eleanor Hull. '

In his collection of humorous sketches, entitled Srorts o INS |
Bogs (Everett and Co.), Mr. Horsley Jebb relates in fine style som ¢
side-splitting experiences in pursuit of the finny and feathered tribe! |
whose habitat is the bog-lands in the north and west. The author 84
clever with his pencil as his pen, and well portrays the curious char®” |
ters with whom he has come in contact.

We warmly welcome another effort to place on record many 111"*er
esting facts of local history and topography in OLp BALLYMENA (Job? |
‘Weir.) This is a reprint of a series of articles that appeared f.:'rigilli‘“’I 3
in the * Ballymenﬁ Observer ”’ in 1857, evidently, the work of a coreld |
student, whose name deserves to be recorded, who was assigted in W‘j_
-f:unf“d.erta.kiﬂg_’ by that splendid scholar and antiquarian—the over-hol |

' ful Bishop Reeves, at the time a humble schoolmaster. The writer g 18

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 43

thrilling deseription of the scenes and incidentqd that marked the out-
o

preak of the ebellion of 1798, and does equal justice to the bravery of
. contending parties and their gallant leaders.

[re. th ; | '
h Mr. Dix indefatigably pursues his valuable researches in Irish
P bibliography, and from time to time we enjoy the fruit of his labours.
th; In his latest work TaBLes Relating to some Dublin Newspapers of the
ory 18th Century (Hanna and Neale) he has put on record, in tabulated
" form, for the assistance of the enquirer, every known copy of the Dublin
| @azette, Faulkner’s and Carson’s Dublin Journals, Pue’s Occurences,
E Reilly’s and Saunder’s News Letters, Sleater’s Gazetter and the Free-
: f man’s Journal, to be found in the seven principal libraries in Dublin.
alT!;E Th's is a good beginning, and we have no doubt it will be followed up
. on similar lines in the future.

Major W. A. Adam, M.P., whose Rus Divinum (8vo., pp. xiv. +
144, Ouseley), has just appeared, seems to be a modern Admirable
Crichton. Born in Dublin in 1865 and educated at Harrow and T.C.D.
he is a scholar, linguist, traveller, warrior, sportsman, prose-writer and
politician. Sighing for fresh worlds to conquer, he here essays to don
: the poet’s laurel. But he hardly succeeds—the Muse is a jealous mis-

Irisg PRIvATE PRrESSES.—I am glad to see that you have discovery
another private press. I have heard of some others, and if any of youy i
readers could supply information concerning them, I should feel obligy

At Achill there was a press the property of the Irish Church Missig

At the Carrogh Orphanage in Co. Kildare, there was another worked i, |
connection with the school there. Cotton states that there was one g T
Rosanna, Co. Wicklow, the residence of Mrs. Tighe, authoress g
‘“ Psyche,” and that the first edition of this poem was printed thereat,
but I have never been able to confirm thfs. There was also one, I haw
heard, at Mount Trenchard, the seat of the Spring Rice family in (o f;
Limerick. The whole subject is well worth careful research, and othens °
may vet be discovered, and their output recorded for the benefit of future

bibliographers and book lovers. E. R. M. D.
Daxter Macrise.—Mr. Ellis’ query (vol. 1, p. 151), reminds me tha

I met a nephew or other near relative of this artist some years agoit
Dublin. His name was Hogg, and he was connected with a one time |
well-known firm of cutlers (in Limerick?) of that name. Ho gave m |
some interesting reminiscences of Maclise, among them being an & |
planation of the extraordinary sameness of that artist’s female figur® |
It seems that Maclise’s unmarried sisters, who lived with him, would
not allow him to introduce a female model into his house. Was not the 2
late Sir John Banks, M.D., connected with Miss Rhoda Banks and Per: §

cival Weldon Banks? 10}, df O’P-

NOTES.

BoxMAHON PRINTING.—Here is a note on this subject'that may be
of interest. I have recently obtained ** An Exposition of the Parables,’
by Benjamin Keach, 1856. Royal 8vo., pp xu1 + 904. It ranges with
the other expository works by him and Gill (10in. by 6.) It 1s evident
from this that there were founts of Greek and Hebrew type used in the
school. Jao:Cl

Deax SWIFT was a frequent visitor to the Achesons (Earl of Gosford)
at Gosford Castle, Markethill, where he thought of renting a farm, and
building a house to be called Drapier’s Hill. Are any of your readers
acquainted with any literature, printed or MS. dealing with Swift’s
stay there, or any Swift-Acheson correspondence ?

Knock. A. RIDDELL.

See Moriarty’s ‘‘ Dean Smf’o and his Writings,”” London, 1893 p.
2%1. Craik’s Life of Swift, ib. 1882, p. 428, and Birkbeck Hill’'s * Un-
published Letters of Dean Swift,”’ ib. N.D. pp. 222 and 243. There were

no Swift MSS. in the Gosford Library Sale 1n April, 1884. ——Ed
